item.title: Ayaneo Slide review: The best compact PC gaming handheld
item.itunes:title: Ayaneo Slide review: The best compact PC gaming handheld
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Ayaneo has been launching some weird and wacky products in recent months, with a ton of gaming handhelds being the vast majority of it. With the Ayaneo 2S, the Ayaneo Kun, the Ayaneo Flip, and the Ayaneo AM01 to name a few, the company has been trying a lot of different things. The Ayaneo Slide is one of the more interesting ones, and surprisingly, it's actually a very good handheld.

The reason I say "surprisingly' is that the Ayaneo Slide is a pretty small handheld, and I wasn't sure what to expect given its smaller screen. As well, one of its biggest features is that it has a keyboard underneath the screen, and the screen has to Slide to reveal it. Hence the name, in case you were wondering. It's a surprisingly decent keyboard that especially aids in setup, though can be extraordinarily useful when playing other games or even just generally using this device.

If what you want is a compact PC gaming handheld, then the Ayaneo Slide is genuinely a fantastic piece of hardware. It feels amazing in the hands, is easily portable, and is surprisingly quiet for the most part with some beefy cooling chops. The small screen mightn't be worth it for some, and I still have some of the same reservations about the Ayaneo brand as a whole that I always have, but it's undoubtedly a great device in its own right. If you were eyeing up a GPD Win 4, this is likely a better option instead.

About this review: I received the Ayaneo Slide for review on the 30th of January, 2024. The company did not have any input into the contents of this review.

Ayaneo Slide
7.5/ 10

The Ayaneo Slide is a gaming handheld with a slidable screen, revealing a backlit keyboard underneath. It packs an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U, up to 64GB of RAM, and up to 4TB of storage.

Pros
  • Sliding keyboard
  • Great performance
  • Ergonomic design
Cons
  • Cheap sliding mechanism
  • Poorer battery life
See at IndieGoGo

Ayaneo Slide: Pricing and availability

The Ayaneo Slide is available on IndieGoGo globally starting at $702. It's available for purchase now and shipping currently, though you'll be importing it from China. At that price, it starts with 16GB of RAM and 512GB of storage.

Ayaneo Slide Specifications
Brand
Ayaneo
Chipset
AMD Ryzen 7 7840U
RAM
Up to 64GB
Storage
Up to 4TB
Headset Compatibility
USB-C, no headphone jack
Display
6-inch 1080p
Ports
2x USB-C
Battery
46.2 WHr

Design and build quality

Compact with a sliding keyboard

Ayaneo has been getting a lot right with its devices, and the company has seemingly settled on a more-or-less consistent design language across it's gaming handhelds. The Ayaneo Slide feels exactly like the Ayaneo 2S but smaller, and that's a great thing. it's comfortable to hold and play for extended sessions, and the weight distribution makes it feel great in the hands. It's covered in soft-touch plastic and incredibly ergonomic.

As always, Ayaneo does a great job on the port options, too. There are two USB-C ports; one on the top, one on the bottom, and both are USB 4 compatibile. There's also a power button with a fingerprint sensor, and a microSD card slot at the bottom. The worst ommission is the lack of a headphone jack, though there are two USB-C to USB-A adapters in the box that you get. There's also no kickstand, but the screen has a neat trick: it can tilt at up to a 30-degree angle.

While Ayaneo devices tend to be well made, the screen tilt itself feels... cheap. There's no tactile feel to it when you tilt it, so you could inadvertently put too much force into it without realising it's already at its limit. As well, the screen itself feels floaty, in that when it's retracted, it's not solidly fixed to the device. I can see this being a painpoint for users, and I worry about the quality control surrounding the spring mechanism that allows the screen to work the way that it does. The keyboard itself also doesn't feel great, but then again, I'm not sure if anyone actually expected it to.

Spring-loaded screen aside, the joysticks are hall effect joysticks (meaning they make use of magnets and won't succumb to drift) that don't have any dead zone issues and the buttons all have a nice, plastic membrane underneath that isn't too mushy. Finally, there's a pair of bottom-firing speakers on the far left and the far right of the device. These are probably the weakest part of the entire package, especially given the lack of a headphone jack.

Related
Ayaneo 2S review: A surprisingly amazing handheld
I wasn't expecting it, but the Ayaneo 2S is probably my favorite gaming handheld yet.

As for why the keyboard is useful, picture this: games like Minecraft are playable on handhelds like these with custom input configurations, but it's not exactly convenient to type in chat. With a handheld like this, you can talk to friends while playing games. For other games that require text input, it'll work very well too. It's a bit of a niche, but it gets the job done, and if you don't want to use it, you don't ever have to look at it thanks to the fact that it's underneath the screen.

The only other downside really is the display. It's fantastic on many accounts with its 1920x1080 resolution, but coming in at 60Hz its significantly behind the competition. It's also pretty small, which is fine, but means that you may miss details in some high-fidelity games. Cyberpunk: 2077 specifically comes to mind here. Honestly, the best part of the Ayaneo Slide isn't the keyboard, but just how compact the overall package is without compromising on performance.

Software

Windows is here to stay

Despite there being rumors of Ayaneo switching to a non-Windows-based OS for an upcoming budget handheld, it seems that the company is sticking to Windows for the foreseeable future... and on all its models. That's fine, but perhaps unsurprisingly, the niche of the keyboard being included here actuallly helps with a lot of the Windows woes.

To be honest, though, the vast majority of what's included here that hasn't been talked to death across all handhelds is the inclusion of Ayaspace. It's a unique-to-Ayaneo software suite used for controlling the TDP and other features of the handheld, both in a dedicated app and an overlay for games. It's one of my favorites on any gaming handheld though, as it gets the job done... and quickly (looking at you, Lenovo).

The Aya Space overlay has undergone a number of enhancements over the last year, offering a broader range of options that are more intuitively accessible. Users can now easily-enough manage settings such as Bluetooth and Wi-Fi, along with adjusting brightness and volume directly through the overlay. While gaming, accessing these features is straightforward and generally operates smoothly. A number of the problems with it have been solved as well, including the frequent crashes I experienced with the Ayaneo Kun.

Performance

The same as all the rest

All of these AMD Ryzen 7 7840U handhelds have one thing in common, and that's performance. If you've used the Ayaneo 2S, the ASUS ROG Ally, the Lenovo Legion Go, or anything else powered by the Z1 Extreme or the 7840U, then you're gonna notice that they all have roughly the same performance level. That's no different here, and it's just the same chip in a different form factor.

Observing the above, the Ayaneo Slide manages to handle Cyberpunk: 2077 perfectly. Running the game's built-in benchmark reveals high 99% and 99.9% frametimes, and from my own experience, the game runs very well using the Steam Deck preset.

With 99.9% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 25.71 FPS and 99% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 30.9 FPS, it's a completely playable experience. We've seen this already with other gaming handhelds though, so it's not too much of a surprise that it works well here, too. The same goes for Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II, which I played online for a couple of minutes to see how it handled it.

The game runs very smoothly as well, with very few stutters throughout my playtest. This is a great way to enjoy Call of Duty on the go, especially given that the game recognizes the Ayaneo's controllers as being a true controller. Because of that, you can queue up against other controller players, without being at a disadvantage to mouse and keyboard players.

With 99.9% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 43.84 FPS and 99% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 67.3 FPS, Call of Duty is an amazing experience on the Ayaneo Slide. I'm playing with the recommended settings out of the box here, and the it looks great and feels amazing in game.

The Ayaneo Slide, like all of the other 7840U handhelds, is extremely capable, and these two games demonstrate it well. Wth its 28W TDP, the Slide is capable of running most games to at least some degree. It's a battery drainer though, and that's somewhat of a downside; with this handheld, the battery is smaller because it itself is smaller. It's not too much of a downgrade thanks to the smaller screen, but it's certainly a noticeable downgrade compared to the Steam Deck OLED or even the Ayaneo 2S. Definitely pick up a power bank for this.

Related
Asus ROG Ally review: Windows makes this difficult to recommend
Plagued by Windows-related problems and quality control issues, the ROG Ally is a fantastic device that's hard to recommend currently.

For indie games, which is a lot of what I've been playing, this is fantastic though. Titles like Hades and Brotato manage to run at much lower performance profiles for hours on end, and that's where it really excels. Overall this handheld is a win in the performance department, especially given its size.

Should you buy the Ayaneo Slide?

You should buy the Ayaneo Slide if:

  • You want a more compact gaming handheld
  • You want something more powerful than the Steam Deck
  • You need a keyboard

You shouldn't buy the Ayaneo Slide if:

  • You worry about aftersales support
  • You don't like Windows
  • You want loud, powerful speakers

The Ayaneo Slide is an excellent gaming handheld that bucks the trend of what they "conventionally" can be. The company has been trying weird and wonderful things with a few of its handhelds, and only second to the Flip, this is the most "out there" yet. If you want a compact gaming handheld with a lot of love that's been put into it, then the Ayaneo Slide is a fantastic option.

However, there's a significant consideration to bear in mind regarding the Ayaneo Slide. Despite it being a top choice (in my opinion) for a gaming handheld, I'm concerned about the after-sales service and potential hardware issues. I had issues with the original Ayaneo 2S, and I've seen other people online having issues too. To be fair to Ayaneo, the gaming community has witnessed instances of companies using social media to undermine rivals, implying that obtaining necessary assistance isn't guaranteed. Moreover, the prospect of returning products to China may pose challenges for some customers encountering issues.

Additionally, Ayaneo's rapid product release cycle is worth noting. While the company commendably offered up an upgrade kit for Ayaneo 2 owners to enhance cooling and audio features at no extra cost when the Ayaneo 2S launched, those who desire the most current technology might find Ayaneo's offerings less appealing. Thankfully, on a performance level, they're all more or less comparable.

Overall, if what you want is a compact gaming handheld, even ignoring the keyboard, this is a fantastic option. However, if you want a keyboard, then this becomes one of the best options available to you on the market. With a great build quality (aside from the cheapness around the screen springs), I definitely feel like this'll be able to take the test of time reasonably well.

Ayaneo Slide
7.5/ 10

The Ayaneo Slide is a gaming handheld with a slidable screen, revealing a backlit keyboard underneath. It packs an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U, up to 64GB of RAM, and up to 4TB of storage.

See at IndieGoGo

That's no different here, and it's just the same chip in a different form factor.

Observing the above, the Ayaneo Slide manages to handle Cyberpunk: 2077 perfectly. Running the game's built-in benchmark reveals high 99% and 99.9% frametimes, and from my own experience, the game runs very well using the Steam Deck preset.

With 99.9% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 25.71 FPS and 99% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 30.9 FPS, it's a completely playable experience. We've seen this already with other gaming handhelds though, so it's not too much of a surprise that it works well here, too. The same goes for Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II, which I played online for a couple of minutes to see how it handled it.

The game runs very smoothly as well, with very few stutters throughout my playtest. This is a great way to enjoy Call of Duty on the go, especially given that the game recognizes the Ayaneo's controllers as being a true controller. Because of that, you can queue up against other controller players, without being at a disadvantage to mouse and keyboard players.

With 99.9% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 43.84 FPS and 99% of frames being drawn at a minimum of 67.3 FPS, Call of Duty is an amazing experience on the Ayaneo Slide. I'm playing with the recommended settings out of the box here, and the it looks great and feels amazing in game.

The Ayaneo Slide, like all of the other 7840U handhelds, is extremely capable, and these two games demonstrate it well. Wth its 28W TDP, the Slide is capable of running most games to at least some degree. It's a battery drainer though, and that's somewhat of a downside; with this handheld, the battery is smaller because it itself is smaller. It's not too much of a downgrade thanks to the smaller screen, but it's certainly a noticeable downgrade compared to the Steam Deck OLED or even the Ayaneo 2S. Definitely pick up a power bank for this.

Related
Asus ROG Ally review: Windows makes this difficult to recommend
Plagued by Windows-related problems and quality control issues, the ROG Ally is a fantastic device that's hard to recommend currently.

For indie games, which is a lot of what I've been playing, this is fantastic though. Titles like Hades and Brotato manage to run at much lower performance profiles for hours on end, and that's where it really excels. Overall this handheld is a win in the performance department, especially given its size.

Should you buy the Ayaneo Slide?

You should buy the Ayaneo Slide if:

  • You want a more compact gaming handheld
  • You want something more powerful than the Steam Deck
  • You need a keyboard

You shouldn't buy the Ayaneo Slide if:

  • You worry about aftersales support
  • You don't like Windows
  • You want loud, powerful speakers

The Ayaneo Slide is an excellent gaming handheld that bucks the trend of what they "conventionally" can be. The company has been trying weird and wonderful things with a few of its handhelds, and only second to the Flip, this is the most "out there" yet. If you want a compact gaming handheld with a lot of love that's been put into it, then the Ayaneo Slide is a fantastic option.

However, there's a significant consideration to bear in mind regarding the Ayaneo Slide. Despite it being a top choice (in my opinion) for a gaming handheld, I'm concerned about the after-sales service and potential hardware issues. I had issues with the original Ayaneo 2S, and I've seen other people online having issues too. To be fair to Ayaneo, the gaming community has witnessed instances of companies using social media to undermine rivals, implying that obtaining necessary assistance isn't guaranteed. Moreover, the prospect of returning products to China may pose challenges for some customers encountering issues.

Additionally, Ayaneo's rapid product release cycle is worth noting. While the company commendably offered up an upgrade kit for Ayaneo 2 owners to enhance cooling and audio features at no extra cost when the Ayaneo 2S launched, those who desire the most current technology might find Ayaneo's offerings less appealing. Thankfully, on a performance level, they're all more or less comparable.

Overall, if what you want is a compact gaming handheld, even ignoring the keyboard, this is a fantastic option. However, if you want a keyboard, then this becomes one of the best options available to you on the market. With a great build quality (aside from the cheapness around the screen springs), I definitely feel like this'll be able to take the test of time reasonably well.

Ayaneo Slide
7.5/ 10

The Ayaneo Slide is a gaming handheld with a slidable screen, revealing a backlit keyboard underneath. It packs an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U, up to 64GB of RAM, and up to 4TB of storage.

See at IndieGoGo

item.title: Alienware's AW3423DWF curved gaming monitor is now $200 off
item.itunes:title: Alienware's AW3423DWF curved gaming monitor is now $200 off
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Alienware QD-OLED AW3423DWF
$800 $1000 Save $200

This gaming monitor uses QD-OLED technology to reach a brightness up to 1,000 nits while having a smooth 165Hz refresh rate and ultra-fast response times of 0.1ms.

$800 at Best Buy

There are a lot of great monitors out there in 2024, which means there are no shortage of options. Whether you're looking for one of the best monitors on the market, affordable options or something in between — there's going to be a monitor out there that fits your budget. With that said, we've found this wonderful deal on Alienware's AW3423WF QD-OLED gaming monitor that's now priced well below original retail, coming in at $200 off for a limited time.

Related
Best curved monitors in 2024
A curved screen can improve immersion, and there's an option for everyone.

What's great about Alienware's 34-inch AW3423DWF monitor?

This is one of the best monitors you're going to find on the market. The Alienware AW3423DWF delivers when it comes to color and performance thanks to its beautiful 34-inch QD-OLED panel that really takes things to another level. While OLED is widely known to offer one of the most vibrant color experiences available, they can sometimes be a little dim if you're in a brightly lit environment.

That's where QD-OLED technology comes into play, with the Alienware monitor cranking brightness up to 1000 nits when necessary, delivering brighter images while still retaining awesome black levels. In addition, you get "cinema-grade color coverage" at DCI-P3 99.3% with image calibration performed at the factory so that the picture quality looks good right out of the box. You also get VESA DisplayHDR TrueBlack 400 certification, along with a refresh rate that tops out at 165Hz.

Furthermore, the monitor supports AMD FreeSync Premium Pro and VESA AdaptiveSync Display certification, which will further enhance image quality even in the most challenging circumstances. The monitor's curved design also provides a level of immersion that is subtle but welcome. As far as the design of the monitor, you get a minimalist design with pops of color on the rear provided by customizable RGB lighting.

There's also cable management on the rear that keeps cables in check, providing a cleaner look when setup on a desk. Of course, Alienware also offers three years of standard warranty coverage with this monitor, which also covers burn-ins if they ever occur. While the price of this monitor isn't cheap, it's one of the best you can buy, providing an amazing viewing experience that really goes unmatched. Just be sure to grab it while you can at its discounted price, because at $799.99, this deal won't last long.

item.title: Best GPUs for video editing in 2024
item.itunes:title: Best GPUs for video editing in 2024
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Content creation has seen a meteoric rise over the past few years. Whether you are just starting out your journey as a casual creator, or are a full-time creative professional, you have to be proficient in video editing. If you prefer to edit your videos on a PC, you will need a dedicated graphics card to expedite the process and increase your productivity. Selecting the ideal video editing graphics card is not a straightforward process, which is why we have curated a list of the very best GPUs that you can use for video editing, depending on your budget and creative workflow.

  • Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC 12G
    Editor's pick
    $770 at Amazon
  • Source: Asus
    ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC Edition
    Premium pick
    $2000 at Newegg
  • Source: Asus
    Asus Dual OC GeForce RTX 3050
    Best budget
    $240 at Amazon
  • Source: Sapphire
    Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX
    Best AMD
    $1030 at Amazon
  • Source: PNY
    PNY RTX 3080 10GB XLR8
    Best For 1440p
    $727 at Amazon
  • Source: MSI
    MSI Ventus 2X GeForce RTX 3060 12GB
    Best For 1440p
    $289 at Newegg
  • Source: Powercolor
    PowerColor Fighter Radeon RX 6750 XT 12GB
    Best value
    $370 at Amazon
  • Source: PNY
    PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB
    Best for professionals
    $4400 at Newegg
Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC 12G
Editor's pick
$770 $900 Save $130

The Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC excels in video editing with its 12GB of GDDR6X memory and 7680 CUDA cores, ensuring smooth 4K editing. Offering solid value and gaming versatility, it's a decent investment for performance-driven users prioritizing reliability and future-proofing.

Pros
  • Excellent editing performance
  • 12GB of GDDR6X memory
  • Superb gaming GPU
Cons
  • High price point
$820 at Best Buy$770 at Amazon

The Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC stands out as the premier choice for video editing due to its cutting-edge features and robust performance. Powered by the latest Ada Lovelace architecture, this GPU delivers unparalleled speed and efficiency, crucial for handling demanding editing tasks. With 12GB of GDDR6X memory, it provides ample VRAM capacity, ensuring smooth editing experiences even with large video files. Its ability to effortlessly manage 4K video editing workflows is a testament to its prowess, making it an ideal companion for professional editors and content creators.

Related
Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC review: Big on size and performance
Don't let this graphics card slide under your radar.

At the heart of its performance lies the 7680 formidable CUDA cores, which serve as the driving force behind its capabilities. These cores enable lightning-fast processing, allowing users to tackle complex editing projects with ease. Furthermore, the inclusion of the 8th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder with AV1 capabilities enhances its versatility, facilitating seamless encoding and decoding of various video formats. Whether it's rendering high-resolution footage or applying intricate effects, the 4070 Ti excels in delivering exceptional results.

Moreover, beyond its prowess in video editing, this particular Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC variant offers remarkable value for money. Its competent cooling solution ensures optimal thermal performance, while its potential for decent overclocking headroom further enhances its capabilities, making it one of the best RTX 4070 Ti variants. Additionally, its suitability for gaming at both 1440p and 4K resolutions underscores its versatility, making it a compelling choice for enthusiasts seeking a GPU that excels in multiple domains.

Despite its premium price tag, the 4070 Ti represents a solid investment for those prioritizing performance, reliability, and future-proofing in their video editing endeavors.

ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC Edition
Premium pick
$2145 $2857 Save $712

For users who want the best of the best without any compromise, the ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC delivers unparalleled video editing performance in 4K and even 8K projects. It has a very steep price point, but it justifies its position in the market thanks to its unique capabilities.

Pros
  • Extraordinary productivity performance
  • Sizeable 24GB memory buffer
  • Quite future proof
Cons
  • Not suitable for SFF or mATX cases
$2145 at Amazon$2000 at Newegg

The ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC is certainly the pinnacle choice for video editing professionals due to its unparalleled performance and modern features. Using NVIDIA’s latest Ada Lovelace architecture, this GPU boasts an impressive arsenal of 24GB of GDDR6X memory, ensuring seamless handling of even the most intricate editing projects. Whether dealing with 3D footage, 4K video, or pushing the boundaries with 8K resolution, the RTX 4090 OC rises to the occasion with ease, thanks to its immense processing power and memory capacity.

Under the hood, a staggering 16384 CUDA cores make up the GPU core, cementing it as the undisputed champion of consumer-grade GPUs in terms of raw computational capability. This powerhouse not only accelerates rendering times but also enhances workflow efficiency, allowing video editors to focus on their creative vision without being hindered by hardware limitations. Furthermore, the inclusion of the 8th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder with AV1 capabilities ensures compatibility with the latest codecs, future-proofing the RTX 4090 OC for upcoming standards.

In addition to its exceptional video editing capabilities, the ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC excels as a versatile multitasker, serving as the ultimate 4K gaming graphics card and adeptly handling animation and productivity tasks. In addition to its premium status and commanding performance, this particular ASUS TUF variant offers a competent cooling solution, a high factory overclock, and ample overclocking headroom.

However, the lofty price tag of the RTX 4090 makes it a luxury reserved for enthusiasts and professionals who demand nothing but the absolute best for their creative pursuits.

Asus Dual OC GeForce RTX 3050
Best budget
$240 $380 Save $140

If you are just starting out in the realm of content creation, the ASUS Dual GeForce RTX 3050 OC might appeal to you. It is a capable video card for 1080p editing on projects with moderate complexity, and its affordable price point helps it stand out as our “budget” pick for this roundup.

Pros
  • Affordable price point
  • Great for entry-level editing
  • Very efficient GPU
Cons
  • Not ideal for complex projects
$240 at Amazon

The ASUS Dual GeForce RTX 3050 OC emerges as the top contender for budget-conscious video editors seeking a capable yet affordable solution. Leveraging the last-gen Ampere architecture, this GPU presents an enticing proposition for those looking to dip their toes into the realm of video editing without breaking the bank. With its 8GB of GDDR6 memory, the RTX 3050 OC provides ample resources to tackle a variety of basic editing tasks, making it an ideal choice for projects targeting 1080p resolution.

Related
Zotac GeForce RTX 3050 Twin Edge OC Edition Review: The best GPU for 1080p gaming?
In this Zotac RTX 3050 review, we're going to take a look at the performance and features of this particular graphics card.

Featuring 2560 CUDA Cores, the RTX 3050 OC delivers commendable performance within its price bracket, ensuring smooth editing experiences without compromising on quality. While it may not boast the sheer computational muscle of higher-end models, it strikes a fine balance between affordability and capability, making it an attractive option for budget-conscious creators. Furthermore, with support for the 7th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder, this GPU facilitates efficient encoding and decoding processes, enhancing workflow efficiency.

While it is a capable choice for video editing, the ASUS Dual GeForce RTX 3050 OC also doubles as a competent gaming GPU, capable of delivering solid 1080p performance in a wide range of titles if you are willing to be flexible with the graphics settings. The compact design of our chosen ASUS Dual variant further adds to its appeal, fitting seamlessly into small form factor PC cases without compromising on functionality.

With its affordability and versatility, the RTX 3050 OC serves as an excellent entry-level option for casual creators, gamers, and value hunters alike, offering a compelling blend of performance and value that's hard to ignore.

Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX
Best AMD
$1030 $1080 Save $50

Team Red’s flagship GPU, the Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX, is certainly not to be ignored when it comes to video editing. Although it does not quite have the software support that NVIDIA enjoys, it still delivers exceptional video editing performance in supported workflows and offers decent value too.

Pros
  • Superb editing performance
  • Decent value for money
  • Exceptional gaming performance
Cons
  • Limited software compatibility
$1030 at Amazon$1030 at Walmart

If you are looking for an option from Team Red, the Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX is the premier choice for video editing enthusiasts seeking the pinnacle of AMD GPU performance. Crafted on the latest AMD RDNA 3 architecture, this GPU sets a new standard for performance and efficiency in the realm of video editing. Boasting a generous 24GB of GDDR6 memory, the RX 7900 XTX stands ready to tackle editing projects of any complexity, whether it's handling 3D footage, 4K resolution, or even testing the waters with 8K content, depending on your workflow of choice.

Related
AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX review: A substantial step-up for RDNA 3
AMD hits back at NVIDIA's mighty RTX 40 series GPUs.

With a formidable array of 6144 stream processors, the RX 7900 XTX stands tall as the most potent AMD GPU currently available to consumers, ensuring lightning-fast rendering times and fluid editing experiences. Its support for the H265/HEVC encoder and decoder, coupled with AV1 capabilities, further enhances its versatility and compatibility with a wide range of video codecs and formats. Additionally, features like AMD Noise Suppression and Smart Access Video provide added capabilities, empowering creators to achieve pristine audiovisual results with ease.

Our chosen variant, the Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX, excels in delivering exceptional overclocking performance and has an efficient cooling solution, ensuring optimal performance even under demanding workloads. From video editing to gaming and productivity tasks, the RX 7900 XTX stands as a versatile powerhouse, capable of elevating creative endeavors to new heights with its unparalleled performance and feature set.

Despite its premium price tag, the RX 7900 XTX offers superior value compared to top-end NVIDIA GPUs, particularly for users whose workflows benefit from AMD GPU acceleration.

PNY RTX 3080 10GB XLR8
Best For 1440p

Even though the PNY GeForce RTX 3080 XLR-8 is a last-gen flagship, it still packs a punch when it comes to its editing performance. It is a great choice for users who work with 1440p video projects, and it can even handle modern games with ease. Its price tag has lowered over the past year which is also a good bonus.

Pros
  • Decent value for money
  • Solid 1440p editing performance
  • Ample 10GB VRAM
Cons
  • High power consumption
$727 at Amazon

The PNY GeForce RTX 3080 XLR8 stands out as the ultimate choice for 1440p video editing, leveraging the formidable power of the last-gen Ampere architecture. With its 10GB of GDDR6X memory, this GPU offers ample resources to handle a wide array of video editing projects, accommodating multiple layers, intricate animations, and complex effects with ease. Its versatility extends to 1440p video editing, delivering exceptional performance while also capable of seamlessly handling 4K editing tasks for relatively lighter projects, providing creators with flexibility and efficiency.

At the core of its performance lies a staggering 10240 CUDA cores, cementing its status as one of the most potent GPUs available on the market. This immense computational power not only accelerates rendering times but also enhances overall workflow efficiency, empowering video editors to tackle demanding projects with confidence. Furthermore, with support for the 7th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder, the GeForce RTX 3080 ensures compatibility with modern encoding and decoding standards, facilitating seamless integration into diverse editing workflows.

Offering benefits beyond video editing, the PNY GeForce RTX 3080 XLR08 excels as one of the fastest 1440p gaming graphics cards on the market, offering a compelling blend of performance and value. With its excellent cooling solution, appealing design, and significant overclocking headroom, the PNY RTX 3080 XLR8 variant emerges as the ultimate choice for creators seeking unparalleled performance and versatility in the realm of 1440p video editing.

Despite its relatively higher initial price point, the introduction of the RTX 40-series by NVIDIA has positioned the RTX 3080 as a mid-range offering, significantly enhancing its value proposition. It now holds a strong position on the market and is a great choice for moderate video editing workloads.

MSI Ventus 2X GeForce RTX 3060 12GB
Best For 1440p
$289 $460 Save $171

NVIDIA’s GeForce RTX 3060 might not be the strongest gaming GPU, but its 12GB variant can be a competent graphics card for 1080p video editing. Its rendering performance is quite strong in its price bracket, and the card is relatively affordable and provides solid value for money.

Pros
  • Large 12GB VRAM buffer
  • Excellent value for money
  • Great 1080p editing performance
Cons
  • Limited future proofing
$289 at Newegg$289 at Amazon

If you are looking for a more affordable option for basic video creation, the MSI GeForce RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X emerges as the optimal choice for 1080p video editing, harnessing the power of the last-gen Ampere architecture to deliver solid performance and value. Opting for the 12GB variant ensures an ample VRAM buffer, essential for handling a multitude of video editing projects with ease. Equipped with 12GB of GDDR6 memory and supported by a 192-bit bus, this GPU provides the necessary resources to tackle complex additions and multiple layers, enabling a seamless editing experience for creators.

Capable of effortlessly handling 1080p video editing tasks and even handling lighter 1440p projects, the RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X strikes a balance between performance and affordability. With 4864 CUDA cores powering its performance, this GPU stands as one of the most competent options available in its price range for video editing enthusiasts. Its support for the 7th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder further enhances its versatility in editing workflows.

On the topic of versatility, the MSI GeForce RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X doubles as a capable 1080p gaming graphics card, offering solid performance across modern titles. Looking at our chosen Ventus 2X variant, its compact form factor makes it an ideal choice for small PC cases, while its basic design and relatively lower price point help its overall value proposition.

Whether for comprehensive 1080p editing tasks or casual video editing at 1440p resolution, the RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X stands as a testament to affordability and performance in the realm of 1080p video editing.

PowerColor Fighter Radeon RX 6750 XT 12GB
Best value

Although the PowerColor Fighter Radeon RX 6750 XT 12GB is quite a forgettable gaming GPU, its editing prowess is quite commendable for the price. The large memory buffer certainly helps here, and it can handle 1080p projects quite comfortably. The low price point and admirable performance highlight its solid value for money.

Pros
  • Affordable price point
  • Sizeable memory buffer
  • Decent gaming performance
Cons
  • Not ideal for complex projects
$370 at Amazon

If you want to maximize your bang for the buck factor, the PowerColor Fighter RX 6750 XT 12GB is a top contender for value-conscious video editors, leveraging the last-gen RDNA 2 architecture to deliver impressive performance and value. Ideal for those seeking budget-friendly editing solutions, this GPU offers a significant advantage with its 12GB of GDDR6 memory, enabling creators to tackle comprehensive editing projects without breaking the bank.

Targeted primarily at 1080p video editing with moderate complexity, the RX 6750 XT 12GB strikes a balance between affordability and capability, making it a great choice for budget-conscious creators. Equipped with 2560 stream processors, the RX 6750 XT 12GB ensures smooth performance and efficient rendering as it cements itself as a competent option for video editing on a budget. Its support for the H265/HEVC encoder and decoder with AV1 support further enhances its compatibility and usability in modern editing timelines.

Additionally, this particular PowerColor Fighter variant offers excellent value for money, featuring a solid cooling solution and a compact design tailored for small form factor PC cases, catering to the needs of creators seeking efficient yet affordable hardware solutions.

Looking beyond its editing capabilities, the PowerColor Fighter RX 6750 XT 12GB is certainly also a reliable gaming GPU, delivering solid performance in 1080p gaming scenarios. With recent price cuts further bolstering its affordability, this GPU stands as an excellent bang for the buck option, appealing to casual creators, gamers, and value hunters alike.

Whether you're just delving into 1080p video editing or seeking a solid gaming experience, the RX 6750 XT 12GB embodies the perfect fusion of performance and affordability, making it our value pick of choice.

PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB
Best for professionals

For creative professionals who value every second of rendering time, the PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB is a fantastic choice for video editing. It falls toward the higher end of NVIDIA’s Quadro lineup and can handle complex productivity tasks with ease. It comes with a very steep price tag though, so its appeal is limited to professionals.

Pros
  • Designed for professionals
  • Unparalleled editing performance
  • Huge 32GB memory buffer
Cons
  • Not suitable for gaming PC builds
$4400 at Newegg

The PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB stands as the epitome of video editing GPUs for professionals, catering specifically to the demands of advanced creative workflows. Positioned within NVIDIA’s Quadro lineup, renowned for its focus on professionals with intricate creative requirements, this GPU is built on the latest Ada Lovelace architecture. Boasting a whopping 32GB of GDDR6 memory, the Quadro RTX 5000 ensures unparalleled capability in handling the most complex editing and productivity projects, from animations to multiple layers, without compromise.

With the sheer processing power of its 12800 CUDA Cores, the Quadro RTX 5000 emerges as a powerhouse GPU tailored for creative professionals. Its ability to effortlessly handle 3D footage, 4K, and 8K video editing tasks speaks volumes about its prowess in delivering unrivaled productivity performance. Adding to its raw computational might, this GPU boasts an industry-leading single-precision performance of 65.3 TFLOPS and a Tensor Core performance of 1044.4 TFLOPS, making it indispensable for rendering, AI, graphics production, animations, and a wide array of compute workloads.

Despite its formidable capabilities, the Quadro RTX 5000 maintains a reasonable Total Board Power (TBP) of 250W, ensuring efficient operation without compromising on performance. This makes it a more suitable choice for dedicated editing workstations than consumer-grade GPUs. However, its steep price tag reflects its professional-grade status, making it an investment reserved for those who prioritize minimizing rendering times and maximizing productivity.

While it may not excel in gaming compared to consumer-grade GPUs, its primary focus on productivity and creative tasks positions it as the ultimate choice for professionals seeking the pinnacle of performance and reliability in their editing workflows.

Choosing the best GPU for video editing

Picking the ideal video editing graphics card for your particular build can be challenging. The choice can vary wildly depending on your editing requirements and workflow, and it can even change based on your video editing software of choice. Moreover, the budget certainly remains the primary factor on which your choice must be based.

If you have a fairly flexible budget and are looking for the ideal video editing graphics card that checks the most boxes, we would recommend the Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC as it is our pick for the “Best Overall” category. The RTX 4070 Ti is certainly capable of handling 1440p and 4K footage with ease and has the latest NVIDIA encoders coupled with 12GB of fast GDDR6X memory. It also provides solid value for money despite being relatively pricey.

Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC 12G
$770 $900 Save $130

The Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC is a powerful GPU that delivers enough performance to run even the most modern AAA titles smoothly. It comes with a small factory overlock out of the box, and has a decent amount of RGB to add a touch of color to your PC.

$770 at Amazon

For users who want the best of the best without any budget limitations, the ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC is our premium pick. Its 24GB of GDDR6X memory is more than enough for most editing projects at 4K or even 8K, and its insanely high core count makes rendering a breeze.

You can also consider the PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB graphics card if you are a creative professional and want a workstation-grade GPU. The Quadro RTX 5000 provides a huge 32GB frame buffer along with industry-leading single-precision and Tensor core performance, making it an amazing workstation GPU for rendering, video encoding, animation work, 3D graphics, and compute workloads.

If you want to maximize your bang for the buck factor, the PowerColor Fighter RX 6750 XT 12GB is a great budget GPU for 1080p video editing. Its large 12GB memory buffer is unique for its price bracket, and its rendering performance is sufficient for most 1080p editing projects of moderate complexity. You will need to make sure that your editing software of choice can take advantage of AMD’s GPU acceleration.

Content creation has seen a meteoric rise over the past few years. Whether you are just starting out your journey as a casual creator, or are a full-time creative professional, you have to be proficient in video editing. If you prefer to edit your videos on a PC, you will need a dedicated graphics card to expedite the process and increase your productivity. Selecting the ideal video editing graphics card is not a straightforward process, which is why we have curated a list of the very best GPUs that you can use for video editing, depending on your budget and creative workflow.

  • Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC 12G
    Editor's pick
    $770 at Amazon
  • Source: Asus
    ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC Edition
    Premium pick
    $2000 at Newegg
  • Source: Asus
    Asus Dual OC GeForce RTX 3050
    Best budget
    $240 at Amazon
  • Source: Sapphire
    Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX
    Best AMD
    $1030 at Amazon
  • Source: PNY
    PNY RTX 3080 10GB XLR8
    Best For 1440p
    $727 at Amazon
  • Source: MSI
    MSI Ventus 2X GeForce RTX 3060 12GB
    Best For 1440p
    $289 at Newegg

    PowerColor Fighter Radeon RX 6750 XT 12GB
    Best value
    $370 at Amazon
  • Source: PNY
    PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB
    Best for professionals
    $4400 at Newegg
Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC 12G
Editor's pick
$770 $900 Save $130

The Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC excels in video editing with its 12GB of GDDR6X memory and 7680 CUDA cores, ensuring smooth 4K editing. Offering solid value and gaming versatility, it's a decent investment for performance-driven users prioritizing reliability and future-proofing.

Pros
  • Excellent editing performance
  • 12GB of GDDR6X memory
  • Superb gaming GPU
Cons
  • High price point
$820 at Best Buy$770 at Amazon

The Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC stands out as the premier choice for video editing due to its cutting-edge features and robust performance. Powered by the latest Ada Lovelace architecture, this GPU delivers unparalleled speed and efficiency, crucial for handling demanding editing tasks. With 12GB of GDDR6X memory, it provides ample VRAM capacity, ensuring smooth editing experiences even with large video files. Its ability to effortlessly manage 4K video editing workflows is a testament to its prowess, making it an ideal companion for professional editors and content creators.

Related
Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC review: Big on size and performance
Don't let this graphics card slide under your radar.

At the heart of its performance lies the 7680 formidable CUDA cores, which serve as the driving force behind its capabilities. These cores enable lightning-fast processing, allowing users to tackle complex editing projects with ease. Furthermore, the inclusion of the 8th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder with AV1 capabilities enhances its versatility, facilitating seamless encoding and decoding of various video formats. Whether it's rendering high-resolution footage or applying intricate effects, the 4070 Ti excels in delivering exceptional results.

Moreover, beyond its prowess in video editing, this particular Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC variant offers remarkable value for money. Its competent cooling solution ensures optimal thermal performance, while its potential for decent overclocking headroom further enhances its capabilities, making it one of the best RTX 4070 Ti variants. Additionally, its suitability for gaming at both 1440p and 4K resolutions underscores its versatility, making it a compelling choice for enthusiasts seeking a GPU that excels in multiple domains.

Despite its premium price tag, the 4070 Ti represents a solid investment for those prioritizing performance, reliability, and future-proofing in their video editing endeavors.

ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC Edition
Premium pick
$2145 $2857 Save $712

For users who want the best of the best without any compromise, the ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC delivers unparalleled video editing performance in 4K and even 8K projects. It has a very steep price point, but it justifies its position in the market thanks to its unique capabilities.

Pros
  • Extraordinary productivity performance
  • Sizeable 24GB memory buffer
  • Quite future proof
Cons
  • Not suitable for SFF or mATX cases
$2145 at Amazon$2000 at Newegg

The ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC is certainly the pinnacle choice for video editing professionals due to its unparalleled performance and modern features. Using NVIDIA’s latest Ada Lovelace architecture, this GPU boasts an impressive arsenal of 24GB of GDDR6X memory, ensuring seamless handling of even the most intricate editing projects. Whether dealing with 3D footage, 4K video, or pushing the boundaries with 8K resolution, the RTX 4090 OC rises to the occasion with ease, thanks to its immense processing power and memory capacity.

Under the hood, a staggering 16384 CUDA cores make up the GPU core, cementing it as the undisputed champion of consumer-grade GPUs in terms of raw computational capability. This powerhouse not only accelerates rendering times but also enhances workflow efficiency, allowing video editors to focus on their creative vision without being hindered by hardware limitations. Furthermore, the inclusion of the 8th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder with AV1 capabilities ensures compatibility with the latest codecs, future-proofing the RTX 4090 OC for upcoming standards.

In addition to its exceptional video editing capabilities, the ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC excels as a versatile multitasker, serving as the ultimate 4K gaming graphics card and adeptly handling animation and productivity tasks. In addition to its premium status and commanding performance, this particular ASUS TUF variant offers a competent cooling solution, a high factory overclock, and ample overclocking headroom.

However, the lofty price tag of the RTX 4090 makes it a luxury reserved for enthusiasts and professionals who demand nothing but the absolute best for their creative pursuits.

Asus Dual OC GeForce RTX 3050
Best budget
$240 $380 Save $140

If you are just starting out in the realm of content creation, the ASUS Dual GeForce RTX 3050 OC might appeal to you. It is a capable video card for 1080p editing on projects with moderate complexity, and its affordable price point helps it stand out as our “budget” pick for this roundup.

Pros
  • Affordable price point
  • Great for entry-level editing
  • Very efficient GPU
Cons
  • Not ideal for complex projects
$240 at Amazon

The ASUS Dual GeForce RTX 3050 OC emerges as the top contender for budget-conscious video editors seeking a capable yet affordable solution. Leveraging the last-gen Ampere architecture, this GPU presents an enticing proposition for those looking to dip their toes into the realm of video editing without breaking the bank. With its 8GB of GDDR6 memory, the RTX 3050 OC provides ample resources to tackle a variety of basic editing tasks, making it an ideal choice for projects targeting 1080p resolution.

Related
Zotac GeForce RTX 3050 Twin Edge OC Edition Review: The best GPU for 1080p gaming?
In this Zotac RTX 3050 review, we're going to take a look at the performance and features of this particular graphics card.

Featuring 2560 CUDA Cores, the RTX 3050 OC delivers commendable performance within its price bracket, ensuring smooth editing experiences without compromising on quality. While it may not boast the sheer computational muscle of higher-end models, it strikes a fine balance between affordability and capability, making it an attractive option for budget-conscious creators. Furthermore, with support for the 7th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder, this GPU facilitates efficient encoding and decoding processes, enhancing workflow efficiency.

While it is a capable choice for video editing, the ASUS Dual GeForce RTX 3050 OC also doubles as a competent gaming GPU, capable of delivering solid 1080p performance in a wide range of titles if you are willing to be flexible with the graphics settings. The compact design of our chosen ASUS Dual variant further adds to its appeal, fitting seamlessly into small form factor PC cases without compromising on functionality.

With its affordability and versatility, the RTX 3050 OC serves as an excellent entry-level option for casual creators, gamers, and value hunters alike, offering a compelling blend of performance and value that's hard to ignore.

Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX
Best AMD
$1030 $1080 Save $50

Team Red’s flagship GPU, the Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX, is certainly not to be ignored when it comes to video editing. Although it does not quite have the software support that NVIDIA enjoys, it still delivers exceptional video editing performance in supported workflows and offers decent value too.

Pros
  • Superb editing performance
  • Decent value for money
  • Exceptional gaming performance
Cons
  • Limited software compatibility
$1030 at Amazon$1030 at Walmart

If you are looking for an option from Team Red, the Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX is the premier choice for video editing enthusiasts seeking the pinnacle of AMD GPU performance. Crafted on the latest AMD RDNA 3 architecture, this GPU sets a new standard for performance and efficiency in the realm of video editing. Boasting a generous 24GB of GDDR6 memory, the RX 7900 XTX stands ready to tackle editing projects of any complexity, whether it's handling 3D footage, 4K resolution, or even testing the waters with 8K content, depending on your workflow of choice.

Related
AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX review: A substantial step-up for RDNA 3
AMD hits back at NVIDIA's mighty RTX 40 series GPUs.

With a formidable array of 6144 stream processors, the RX 7900 XTX stands tall as the most potent AMD GPU currently available to consumers, ensuring lightning-fast rendering times and fluid editing experiences. Its support for the H265/HEVC encoder and decoder, coupled with AV1 capabilities, further enhances its versatility and compatibility with a wide range of video codecs and formats. Additionally, features like AMD Noise Suppression and Smart Access Video provide added capabilities, empowering creators to achieve pristine audiovisual results with ease.

Our chosen variant, the Sapphire Nitro+ Radeon RX 7900 XTX, excels in delivering exceptional overclocking performance and has an efficient cooling solution, ensuring optimal performance even under demanding workloads. From video editing to gaming and productivity tasks, the RX 7900 XTX stands as a versatile powerhouse, capable of elevating creative endeavors to new heights with its unparalleled performance and feature set.

Despite its premium price tag, the RX 7900 XTX offers superior value compared to top-end NVIDIA GPUs, particularly for users whose workflows benefit from AMD GPU acceleration.

PNY RTX 3080 10GB XLR8
Best For 1440p

Even though the PNY GeForce RTX 3080 XLR-8 is a last-gen flagship, it still packs a punch when it comes to its editing performance. It is a great choice for users who work with 1440p video projects, and it can even handle modern games with ease. Its price tag has lowered over the past year which is also a good bonus.

Pros
  • Decent value for money
  • Solid 1440p editing performance
  • Ample 10GB VRAM
Cons
  • High power consumption
$727 at Amazon

The PNY GeForce RTX 3080 XLR8 stands out as the ultimate choice for 1440p video editing, leveraging the formidable power of the last-gen Ampere architecture. With its 10GB of GDDR6X memory, this GPU offers ample resources to handle a wide array of video editing projects, accommodating multiple layers, intricate animations, and complex effects with ease. Its versatility extends to 1440p video editing, delivering exceptional performance while also capable of seamlessly handling 4K editing tasks for relatively lighter projects, providing creators with flexibility and efficiency.

At the core of its performance lies a staggering 10240 CUDA cores, cementing its status as one of the most potent GPUs available on the market. This immense computational power not only accelerates rendering times but also enhances overall workflow efficiency, empowering video editors to tackle demanding projects with confidence. Furthermore, with support for the 7th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder, the GeForce RTX 3080 ensures compatibility with modern encoding and decoding standards, facilitating seamless integration into diverse editing workflows.

Offering benefits beyond video editing, the PNY GeForce RTX 3080 XLR08 excels as one of the fastest 1440p gaming graphics cards on the market, offering a compelling blend of performance and value. With its excellent cooling solution, appealing design, and significant overclocking headroom, the PNY RTX 3080 XLR8 variant emerges as the ultimate choice for creators seeking unparalleled performance and versatility in the realm of 1440p video editing.

Despite its relatively higher initial price point, the introduction of the RTX 40-series by NVIDIA has positioned the RTX 3080 as a mid-range offering, significantly enhancing its value proposition. It now holds a strong position on the market and is a great choice for moderate video editing workloads.

MSI Ventus 2X GeForce RTX 3060 12GB
Best For 1440p
$289 $460 Save $171

NVIDIA’s GeForce RTX 3060 might not be the strongest gaming GPU, but its 12GB variant can be a competent graphics card for 1080p video editing. Its rendering performance is quite strong in its price bracket, and the card is relatively affordable and provides solid value for money.

Pros
  • Large 12GB VRAM buffer
  • Excellent value for money
  • Great 1080p editing performance
Cons
  • Limited future proofing
$289 at Newegg$289 at Amazon

If you are looking for a more affordable option for basic video creation, the MSI GeForce RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X emerges as the optimal choice for 1080p video editing, harnessing the power of the last-gen Ampere architecture to deliver solid performance and value. Opting for the 12GB variant ensures an ample VRAM buffer, essential for handling a multitude of video editing projects with ease. Equipped with 12GB of GDDR6 memory and supported by a 192-bit bus, this GPU provides the necessary resources to tackle complex additions and multiple layers, enabling a seamless editing experience for creators.

Capable of effortlessly handling 1080p video editing tasks and even handling lighter 1440p projects, the RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X strikes a balance between performance and affordability. With 4864 CUDA cores powering its performance, this GPU stands as one of the most competent options available in its price range for video editing enthusiasts. Its support for the 7th Gen NVENC encoder and 5th Gen NVDEC decoder further enhances its versatility in editing workflows.

On the topic of versatility, the MSI GeForce RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X doubles as a capable 1080p gaming graphics card, offering solid performance across modern titles. Looking at our chosen Ventus 2X variant, its compact form factor makes it an ideal choice for small PC cases, while its basic design and relatively lower price point help its overall value proposition.

Whether for comprehensive 1080p editing tasks or casual video editing at 1440p resolution, the RTX 3060 12GB Ventus 2X stands as a testament to affordability and performance in the realm of 1080p video editing.

PowerColor Fighter Radeon RX 6750 XT 12GB
Best value

Although the PowerColor Fighter Radeon RX 6750 XT 12GB is quite a forgettable gaming GPU, its editing prowess is quite commendable for the price. The large memory buffer certainly helps here, and it can handle 1080p projects quite comfortably. The low price point and admirable performance highlight its solid value for money.

Pros
  • Affordable price point
  • Sizeable memory buffer
  • Decent gaming performance
Cons
  • Not ideal for complex projects
$370 at Amazon

If you want to maximize your bang for the buck factor, the PowerColor Fighter RX 6750 XT 12GB is a top contender for value-conscious video editors, leveraging the last-gen RDNA 2 architecture to deliver impressive performance and value. Ideal for those seeking budget-friendly editing solutions, this GPU offers a significant advantage with its 12GB of GDDR6 memory, enabling creators to tackle comprehensive editing projects without breaking the bank.

Targeted primarily at 1080p video editing with moderate complexity, the RX 6750 XT 12GB strikes a balance between affordability and capability, making it a great choice for budget-conscious creators. Equipped with 2560 stream processors, the RX 6750 XT 12GB ensures smooth performance and efficient rendering as it cements itself as a competent option for video editing on a budget. Its support for the H265/HEVC encoder and decoder with AV1 support further enhances its compatibility and usability in modern editing timelines.

Additionally, this particular PowerColor Fighter variant offers excellent value for money, featuring a solid cooling solution and a compact design tailored for small form factor PC cases, catering to the needs of creators seeking efficient yet affordable hardware solutions.

Looking beyond its editing capabilities, the PowerColor Fighter RX 6750 XT 12GB is certainly also a reliable gaming GPU, delivering solid performance in 1080p gaming scenarios. With recent price cuts further bolstering its affordability, this GPU stands as an excellent bang for the buck option, appealing to casual creators, gamers, and value hunters alike.

Whether you're just delving into 1080p video editing or seeking a solid gaming experience, the RX 6750 XT 12GB embodies the perfect fusion of performance and affordability, making it our value pick of choice.

PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB
Best for professionals

For creative professionals who value every second of rendering time, the PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB is a fantastic choice for video editing. It falls toward the higher end of NVIDIA’s Quadro lineup and can handle complex productivity tasks with ease. It comes with a very steep price tag though, so its appeal is limited to professionals.

Pros
  • Designed for professionals
  • Unparalleled editing performance
  • Huge 32GB memory buffer
Cons
  • Not suitable for gaming PC builds
$4400 at Newegg

The PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB stands as the epitome of video editing GPUs for professionals, catering specifically to the demands of advanced creative workflows. Positioned within NVIDIA’s Quadro lineup, renowned for its focus on professionals with intricate creative requirements, this GPU is built on the latest Ada Lovelace architecture. Boasting a whopping 32GB of GDDR6 memory, the Quadro RTX 5000 ensures unparalleled capability in handling the most complex editing and productivity projects, from animations to multiple layers, without compromise.

With the sheer processing power of its 12800 CUDA Cores, the Quadro RTX 5000 emerges as a powerhouse GPU tailored for creative professionals. Its ability to effortlessly handle 3D footage, 4K, and 8K video editing tasks speaks volumes about its prowess in delivering unrivaled productivity performance. Adding to its raw computational might, this GPU boasts an industry-leading single-precision performance of 65.3 TFLOPS and a Tensor Core performance of 1044.4 TFLOPS, making it indispensable for rendering, AI, graphics production, animations, and a wide array of compute workloads.

Despite its formidable capabilities, the Quadro RTX 5000 maintains a reasonable Total Board Power (TBP) of 250W, ensuring efficient operation without compromising on performance. This makes it a more suitable choice for dedicated editing workstations than consumer-grade GPUs. However, its steep price tag reflects its professional-grade status, making it an investment reserved for those who prioritize minimizing rendering times and maximizing productivity.

While it may not excel in gaming compared to consumer-grade GPUs, its primary focus on productivity and creative tasks positions it as the ultimate choice for professionals seeking the pinnacle of performance and reliability in their editing workflows.

Choosing the best GPU for video editing

Picking the ideal video editing graphics card for your particular build can be challenging. The choice can vary wildly depending on your editing requirements and workflow, and it can even change based on your video editing software of choice. Moreover, the budget certainly remains the primary factor on which your choice must be based.

If you have a fairly flexible budget and are looking for the ideal video editing graphics card that checks the most boxes, we would recommend the Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC as it is our pick for the “Best Overall” category. The RTX 4070 Ti is certainly capable of handling 1440p and 4K footage with ease and has the latest NVIDIA encoders coupled with 12GB of fast GDDR6X memory. It also provides solid value for money despite being relatively pricey.

Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC 12G
$770 $900 Save $130

The Gigabyte GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Gaming OC is a powerful GPU that delivers enough performance to run even the most modern AAA titles smoothly. It comes with a small factory overlock out of the box, and has a decent amount of RGB to add a touch of color to your PC.

$770 at Amazon

For users who want the best of the best without any budget limitations, the ASUS TUF Gaming GeForce RTX 4090 OC is our premium pick. Its 24GB of GDDR6X memory is more than enough for most editing projects at 4K or even 8K, and its insanely high core count makes rendering a breeze.

You can also consider the PNY Quadro RTX 5000 32GB graphics card if you are a creative professional and want a workstation-grade GPU. The Quadro RTX 5000 provides a huge 32GB frame buffer along with industry-leading single-precision and Tensor core performance, making it an amazing workstation GPU for rendering, video encoding, animation work, 3D graphics, and compute workloads.

If you want to maximize your bang for the buck factor, the PowerColor Fighter RX 6750 XT 12GB is a great budget GPU for 1080p video editing. Its large 12GB memory buffer is unique for its price bracket, and its rendering performance is sufficient for most 1080p editing projects of moderate complexity. You will need to make sure that your editing software of choice can take advantage of AMD’s GPU acceleration.
